speaker: interviewer
text: It's Tuesday, the 14th of November. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Fred Knoller in London, England. This is take five. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard-- we heard rumors all the time. We heard that the Allied troops invaded-- went in-- not invaded but went into France. And they-- they-- that we heard towards the end of 1944 the defeats by the Germans on the Russian front. And the joy and the-- the-- the pleasure we got out of it, it was tremendous. But again, we didn't have newspapers. We only heard it from people who maybe were working with the SS, where they had radios. And-- and-- but again, it was German radios. But we knew there were fights. The Russians were approaching. Towards the end of October, November, we got bombed. We were-- we got bombed by the Americans while we were working in Buna. They were bombing the factory, and we were in it, and it was, again, quite traumatic-- traumatic. But it was-- we were elated, because we felt that this was the beginning of the end. We heard that the Russians approaching into Poland. We had another raid by Russian planes. It was bombed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- the selections, where more and more were selected to be gassed-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In December, 1944, we were told that we are evacuating. We heard artillery already from the east, sound of artillery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If I am not mistaken, it was either end of December or maybe beginning January. I do not remember the date. We were told to assemble, and we were told that we were going to walk out in columns of five, and that we are walking, which I knew later on it was towards Katowice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was winter. Snow. It was freezing cold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- at work, I organized myself. I stole some cement bags, paper, which carried this-- cement was in it. And I put on this cement bag underneath my pajamas, striped clothes, on-- onto the body, because-- hoping that this would us-- protect me a little bit against the cold. It was-- I think it was very lucky that I had this, because it did somehow help. And we walked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all day. I remember we stopped in a cegla factory, in-- in a brick factory in Gleiwitz. I think it was Gleiwitz, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one surrounded by SS. One of the-- from our group tried to escape. SS shot him. We continued walking and walking. I believe we walked somewhere around 80 kilometers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was freezing cold. We want-- went on the road, tree-- trees and woods on either side. Nothing to eat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Absolutely nothing to eat. We picked up snow, put the snow into our mouths to get some liquid. And people collapsed. Soon--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sort of personal state of mind at-- at this point? Were you very weakened by everything that had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I weakened. I mean, you don't-- I don't think except-- The thought was that I wanted to survive. I saw them shooting people who collapsed. I wanted to make sure that I don't collapse. And to walk this amount with wooden shoes, wooden soles, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your feet were blood. Must be blood, because I felt some liquid in there from walking. And I had here, on both arms, you know, eczemas, or-- don't know-- which never healed. In fact, I had it for even months, months afterwards. Just doesn't-- didn't heal. And we walked, and people, one-- a few, run into the woods, thought-- thought to [INAUDIBLE]. But again, SS shot them. And when people collapsed, they were shot. There was will in me. I must survive. And I really, really walked and walked. This was the famous death march that we experienced. And the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] feeling of all of us was just-- I'm positive that we lost thousands on that death march. We arrived in Katowice. They put us on the train, again, in wagons. Closed the door, and we went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a day and a night. Actually we did get some hot water, black water or something there. Still no food. We collapsed in the train. We were not 100 in each wagon. We were less. Somehow we were able to lie down. And we were there one whole day. Arrived in a camp, which was called Dora, Nordhausen, at the station. Was Nordhausen was the name of the station. We were put in barracks there, and we were told that we were going to work underneath the mountains. There's a factory in the mountains. It was the Harzgebirge, the Harz Mountain, which was near Buchenwald. And we were, again, guarded by SS officers. Now, when we went to work the next day, into the mountain, into the tunnels of the mountains, we noticed machineries being fabricated, big machines. Over there they produced the V-1 and the V-2. This was produced in Dora, in that factory underneath the mountain, the V1 that you all know so well. We had to carry heavy metal things. We had to dig-- dig in the roads, et cetera. We were to work, again, very hard. We had couples who guided us. But a lot of prisoners were working on the machineries. When we pushed these V-1 machines from one place to another, one of the engineers, a Jewish prisoner, told us, pick up some sand and put it in there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At least these bombs are not going to explode. And he told us we are to put the sand-- We were part of a sabotage group. One day, a group was caught doing that. Not our group. And they were interrogated. And the engineer who told us where to put-- and his other engineers who were working on the machinery-- were put right away, without any trial, a rope around their neck and hung on the-- on the moving-- from the ceiling. They had these hooks-- and were hung in front of us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was somewhere around January, February. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] End of February, we heard again artillery. We were told the Americans coming up from the south towards Dora. We were put in trains again, and we were transported to a place near Hamburg, Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said 'we' many-- many times. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with the same people all the time? Did you get to know these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were always new people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always new people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got put in barracks. We couldn't choose with whom we were going, we were put in. Guards-- who stayed there, there, and there, and we didn't have-- I didn't-- we were considering ourselves part of a group. It was a new group that we never really saw before. Some of them we saw, and some of them we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't develop relationships?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no relationship whatsoever between us, except that we worked together and slept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on the train again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Bergen-Belsen. We were put into SS barracks, because the SS were hardly there anymore. That was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of March. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we did not go to work anymore. We didn't get-- hardly didn't get any food anymore. Maybe we got some hot soup. There was no bread available. We dug the-- we dug the earth for roots or something. A lot of people just died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were just wandering around. We heard, again, artillery coming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of people died on the ground. The SS disappeared. There were no SS. We were-- we were guarded by Hungarian and Croatian soldiers with guns, anti-Semitic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people just-- the hunger that we experienced is just unbelievable. When you go and dig up for roots, you know what it-- what it can do to you. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Finally, on the 15th of April, two days before my birthday, the British troop entered. We were sitting-- I remember, we were sitting. I couldn't walk very much. Sitting on the ground, and we saw these troops coming in. You would imagine that everybody would be shouting, hurray, we're free! It was absolutely quiet. Maybe because we couldn't believe that this is the end. Maybe because we didn't have the strength to shout. Maybe we-- because we only can think of food. We couldn't think of anything else. Food, food, food. All during the two years, hunger, that was our thought. You got to eat to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember there was a Jewish chaplain of the British army. I later knew it was Reverend Hardman from Hendon synagogue. We met and talked about it quite often. We were given our first hot food, but it was a big mistake. What they did, they gave us milk and rice with sugar, sweet. The people were grabbing at it and eating as much as they could. And they died. A lot of them died because their stomach just couldn't-- their body couldn't take it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One doctor warned us, eat it slowly, and don't eat much. And I took note of him, what he said. I just wanted to satisfy somehow or other my hunger. But I stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Surrounding it, I remember, surrounding where we sat there when they approached, a lot of bodies. A lot of bodies were there. I [SIGHS] wanted to talk to an English officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said to him that I-- I've a brother who I believe is in England and another brother who's in America. He said, don't worry. We'll take note of all that here. Next day, we were interviewed, written down everything. I don't know if it was the next day or somehow a few days later. At one time, the army could not provide us with-- with food. So they organized of people who were able to walk to get a wheelbarrow, and said, go into the village. Go-- go into houses and take from the Germans, and we'll go with you. We will go with you. The-- the English soldiers, two soldiers came with us, and we will take from the Germans food for the camp. And we loaded up big ham and salami and-- and rice and whatever we-- flour and everything. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] A lot of people showing off showed me they were taking money. There were diamonds with-- you know. I only was thinking of food. And when I went into one of these houses, I saw hidden away, underneath a board, a picture of a-- of Hitler, Adolf Hitler, in a frame, beautiful frame. That took my-- tore up this here. And one of the old men-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] 'You dirty Jew.' So I went to him, and I hit that old man until he collapsed. And the English soldier said, I don't blame you for doing that, because I told him what he-- what he called out to me. And I always remember the face of this old man. You know, he bled, blood coming from him, but I just couldn't stop myself to hit him. And I hit him, and I hit him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe my whole frustration came out. How dare he? We are free now. He still calls us [GERMAN], a dirty pig, Jew pig. And it's something I'm not too proud of. I believe I killed him. I believe I killed him. We left, took all the food that we wanted, went back on the camp, and it was distributed. And again, [SIGHS] it's-- we were questioned, asked where we want to go. I was told, oh, the-- the organization, a world organization, Joint. Yeah, Joint was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Women interviewing us, where we wanted to go, et cetera. And I said, look, I left my parents in Vienna. I said, it's no use to go to Vienna, because there isn't a Jew alive in Vienna. There is no Jew living there. They were-- everybody was deported. I said, OK, I will go back to see whether I can find my cousins in France. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fine. I was taken by plane back to France. The French government took care of us. They put us in a hotel in Paris-- in Paris, Hotel Raspail in-- that's near Montparnasse, Boulevard Raspail. And we were in the hotel. All the deportee, had-- each one had their own room. And we were really taken as VIPs. We were interviewed by the French government. We got new identification papers. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they asked about family, et cetera. I told him that I knew that my brother went to America. He said, look, what you must do, you must go to the American embassy and tell him that you have a brother in America and see, maybe they can find him. This is what I did. And I went-- and I remember her, an old vice president-- vice counsel, Mrs. Dix was her name. And I told her my story, nearly cried. And I told her that my brother went to Florida. He got a job in Florida at a canning factory. His name is Eric Knoller. I said-- she said, look, we will find him. I can assure you we will find him. You gave us enough information to find him. I gave them the age, et cetera. And-- but you must tell us if you don't-- if you go away from the Hotel Raspail, you must tell us where you are. I went back to the hotel. The government who interviewed us, the French government, said, look-- to me, when they interviewed-- you must go-- you're very thin. You must go recuperate. We want you to go to a place that we are going to arrange for you, out in the country. And [SIGHS] they sent me to a Dr. [? Bennetin. ?] It was a little village, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a little village in central France. I've forgotten the name. And the doctor took me in. They were very devout Christians. And they really took care of me. In fact, as soon I came, he said, do me a favor. Put on your uniform-- because I still had my uniform, the pajama-- I want to take a picture for-- for posterity. And I have these photos. And as you can see, I still had my hair shaved-- shaven. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- I was really taken care of in that village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day I was walking with the chemist's daughter of the village, for a walk in the village. Suddenly, I see an American Jeep. I mean, hardly-- I mean, such a small village, you hardly see a car. It was an American Jeep. And out of the Jeep, an American soldier in uniform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Looked at him. It was my brother, my brother Eric. I-- because I told Mrs. Dix, the consul, where I was going, where I'm going to stay. She found that he was in the American Army, and she told him where I was. His commanding officer-- by the way, his commanding officer gave him-- he was in all concentration camps because-- to find me. And he gave him a Jeep and another guy with him to search me. And he found out that I was alive somewhere or other. And finally found where I was, and he came to that village. And it was an amazing reunion. I mean-- [LAUGHS] If I still think about that, I get tears in my eyes. He told me that he went to search for all of us. He went to Vienna. The parents were not anymore there. Were not-- And they couldn't find my parents. He doesn't know what happened to them. He told me that my other brother, Otto, who was in England in a camp just before the war started, got-- he got my brother an affidavit to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went-- Otto went to America, and he studied medicine in America, and he became a doctor. And we both notified, sent a telegram to my brother Otto, that I'm alive and, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- we went back to Paris. My brother Eric, he had to go back to his unit. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to the Hotel Raspail. I must say, the French government really took care of the deportee. And now we were part of an association of deportee, internee, you know, and-- and we were quite VIPs in France, walking around. Although I know how-- now I know how much French people collaborated with the-- with the Germans. But naturally, nobody was a collaborator afterwards. They were all anti-Germans when we met them, when we talked about it. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eric told me he's making immediately arrangements for me to get affidavits and visas to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was liberated in 1945. I-- After liberation, I went to Limoges and found that my uncle and aunt are still alive, but the children were not there. Only the eldest daughter, Rosie, was there. And I stayed with them. We were, again, together in Limoges. And suddenly, [? Maxl ?] and Leo, my cousins, arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And their story was that they were arrested in the street as French people. And they were taken to Germany to work. And they told them that we're French, and we are-- they more or less got them to work for Germany. So they were not prisoners, not in-- not in concentration camps, but they were workers, French foreign workers. And this is how they survived. And they came back to France, and we met again in Limoges. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There I met my very close friends-- in fact, we lived together-- Leo [? Bretholz, ?] with whom I am still today, at least we see each other once a year, or we write to each other once a month. We are very close. He lives in Baltimore. Anyhow, we went-- he also got an affidavit to go to America. And in 1947, we both went on the same boat, [? Leo Bretholz ?] and myself. Went to America. I went to New York because my oldest brother lived in New York. And he went to his aunt. He had an aunt in Baltimore, Maryland. I worked for a year in New York, learning English at night classes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I was a packer in some firm. Then my friend Leo told me to join him in Baltimore, Maryland, and he has a job for me in the same firm where he works, in a textile firm. So I was quite happy to go to Baltimore. And we, again, had our apartment together, and lived there with Leo. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a-- it's a-- it was a joy, really, to be together with a big group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make a long story short, really, it is-- life started to get normal again. Suddenly, somebody comes to me and said, hey, do you want to meet somebody from the old country? I said, sure, I don't mind meeting somebody from the old country. Because in America, every Saturday you had to have a date. You had to have a date. So I phoned up my-- she gave me a phone number. She said, phone up that girl from the old country. [SIGHS] This is how I make a date over the telephone with somebody called Freda Bloom. And we spoke for one hour on the telephone. For one hour, we spoke. And we made a date. And I said to my friend Leo, Leo, this girl, I'm going to marry her. He said, you must be mad. You never met her. Maybe she looks like a dog. Why-- how can you say that? No. This is the girl for me. We met, and we fell in love with each other. We met in October, got engaged in November, and got married on the 31st of December, the last day of the year. Why the last day? Because the American government gave us-- as if we were married for the whole year-- we got a present of somewhere around $400.
